Average oxygen device level in 0–12h reflects respiratory support needs immediately post-op. It is an indirect severity/oxygenation proxy with limited renal specificity and early transient effects.

Mean oxygen device requirement in 13–24h captures persistent respiratory support needs beyond the immediate post-op phase. Renal linkage is indirect (overall illness/oxygen delivery), so modest importance.

Mean oxygen device requirement in 25–36h reflects sustained respiratory failure/ongoing support close to Hour 60, which can correlate with hemodynamic stress and AKI risk. Still indirect and partly treatment-driven, so not top-tier.

Maximum oxygen device level in 0–12h captures peak early respiratory support, often influenced by routine post-op ventilation/weaning. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy for severity with limited renal specificity.

Peak oxygen device requirement in 13–24h may indicate respiratory deterioration and higher overall acuity. Association with creatinine rise is indirect (hypoxemia/instability), so remains low-moderate.

Peak oxygen device requirement in 25–36h is a closer leading indicator of ongoing respiratory failure and systemic stress that can accompany renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Extreme (max) captures clinically meaningful deterioration better than mean, but remains indirect.
Minimum oxygen device level in 0–12h largely reflects brief periods of lower support during routine post-op transitions. It has little renal specificity and the minimum is not a strong insult marker.

Minimum oxygen device requirement in 13–24h mainly reflects best observed respiratory status rather than hypoxic burden. It is weakly related to renal outcomes and is not an insult-focused aggregation.

Minimum oxygen device requirement in 25–36h can indicate whether the patient ever achieves low support close to the outcome window, modestly reflecting recovery trajectory. Still indirect and less informative than max/mean for capturing respiratory stress.
Frequency of documenting oxygen device in 0–12h is primarily a monitoring/workflow signal. It is not kidney-specific and is heavily confounded by routine post-op care.
Oxygen device measurement frequency in 13–24h reflects clinician attention and respiratory therapy workflow more than physiology. Proxy penalty dominates with minimal direct renal relevance.
More frequent oxygen device documentation in 25–36h may signal ongoing respiratory instability closer to Hour 60. However it remains a care-process proxy, so importance is limited.

Average chest tube output reflects postoperative bleeding/mediastinal drainage and overall hemodynamic stress. It is only indirectly related to AKI via hypovolemia/transfusion and early values are often transient, so predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is limited.

Mean chest tube output in 13–24h suggests ongoing bleeding/inflammation and may correlate with resuscitation needs. Renal linkage remains indirect and the mean can dilute short high-output episodes most relevant to renal hypoperfusion.

Persistently elevated mean chest tube output at 25–36h can indicate continued losses and potential hypoperfusion closer to the outcome window. Still a nonspecific proxy for illness/bleeding management rather than a kidney-specific mechanism.

Peak early chest tube output captures acute bleeding that can drive hypotension, transfusion, and renal hypoperfusion—pathways linked to AKI. Max is the better aggregation for insult detection, but immediate post-op peaks are common and not always prognostic.

A high maximum chest tube output in 13–24h suggests ongoing bleeding/tamponade risk and greater likelihood of hemodynamic compromise affecting renal perfusion. Still indirect and partly reflects surgical/management factors rather than kidney injury itself.

A late peak chest tube output (25–36h) is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may signal continued losses with renal hypoperfusion risk. It remains a proxy variable, but max appropriately captures clinically meaningful extremes.

Minimum chest tube output is usually near zero and is not a meaningful marker of bleeding severity or renal risk. It adds little beyond noise and does not capture the relevant extreme (high output) physiology.
Minimum chest tube output in this window is typically uninformative and may reflect measurement gaps or brief low drainage. It has minimal mechanistic connection to AKI prediction.
Minimum chest tube output late post-op is generally not clinically interpretable for renal risk and is often zero. It is unlikely to improve prediction of creatinine ratio.
This reflects how often chest tube output was recorded, primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinical workflow signal. It is not a renal mechanism and early post-op measurement frequency is routine.
Measurement count mainly captures nursing/ICU monitoring practices and possibly concern about bleeding. It is an indirect proxy with weak specificity for subsequent creatinine rise.
Late measurement frequency is still largely a care-process proxy rather than physiology. It should not be a strong predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean pulmonary artery systolic pressure reflects RV afterload/volume status and can relate indirectly to renal congestion or low cardiac output. Early post-op PAP is often influenced by ventilation/anesthesia and mean values may miss brief hemodynamic insults.

Sustained elevated PAP in 13–24h can indicate RV dysfunction/pulmonary hypertension with venous congestion, which is a plausible contributor to AKI. This window is more prognostic than immediate post-op, though mean still smooths extremes.

Mean PAP systolic at 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may capture ongoing RV strain and renal venous congestion affecting GFR. It remains indirect (not a kidney biomarker) but has reasonable physiologic linkage.

Peak PAP systolic early may capture episodes of acute RV afterload/ventilator-related pulmonary hypertension that can reduce forward flow and worsen renal perfusion. Max better captures harmful extremes, but early post-op spikes can be transient and nonspecific.

Peak systolic pulmonary artery pressure reflects RV afterload/pulmonary hypertension and can correlate with venous congestion affecting renal perfusion. It is an indirect, moderately proximal signal and this mid-epoch max is less predictive than later windows.

Late-window peak systolic PAP may capture ongoing RV strain and venous congestion, which can contribute to cardiorenal syndrome and AKI risk. Temporal proximity is good and the max captures clinically meaningful extremes, but renal specificity remains indirect.

Minimum systolic PAP in the immediate post-op period is often influenced by anesthesia/ventilation and transient hemodynamic swings. It is not kidney-specific and early minima are weak, noisy proxies for later creatinine rise.

Low systolic PAP may reflect low flow states or measurement variability rather than a specific renal injury pathway. As an indirect and potentially noisy minimum in a mid window, it has limited incremental value for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

A late-window low systolic PAP could indicate low cardiac output/low pulmonary flow states that may reduce renal perfusion. Temporal proximity helps, but it remains an indirect marker and minima are less clearly tied to renal congestion than elevated pressures.

Count/frequency of PAP systolic measurements mainly reflects presence of a PA catheter and monitoring intensity rather than physiology. This is a care-process proxy with low renal mechanistic relevance.

Measurement frequency is primarily a proxy for clinician concern/instability and PA catheter use, not a direct renal risk factor. It may weakly correlate with severity but is nonspecific for predicting creatinine ratio.
Late continued frequent PAP monitoring can indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability, which may relate to AKI risk. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.

Mean diastolic PAP approximates pulmonary vascular load and can relate to right-sided filling pressures/venous congestion impacting kidneys. In the immediate post-op window, mean values may wash out brief insults and are less informative.

Sustained diastolic PAP elevation can reflect ongoing RV dysfunction and congestion, indirectly affecting renal function. Mid-epoch mean is moderately relevant but not kidney-specific and may dilute extremes.

Late-window mean diastolic PAP may capture sustained pulmonary hypertension/RV failure with venous congestion, a plausible contributor to worsening renal function. Good temporal proximity supports a higher score despite indirect renal specificity.

Peak diastolic PAP may reflect episodes of high pulmonary pressures and congestion that can impair renal venous outflow. Early post-op peaks are common/transient, so predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine is limited.

Mid-window peak diastolic PAP can indicate intermittent RV afterload/congestion, which is an indirect AKI pathway. The max captures extremes, but renal linkage is still secondary and timing is not the closest.

Late-window peak diastolic PAP may signal ongoing or worsening pulmonary hypertension/RV failure with congestion, which can precipitate cardiorenal AKI. Temporal proximity and extreme aggregation support moderate-strong usefulness, though still indirect.

Minimum diastolic PAP early after surgery is heavily influenced by transient perioperative factors and measurement noise. It has weak renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity to later creatinine rise.

Low diastolic PAP is an indirect and nonspecific marker that may reflect low flow or ventilatory effects rather than renal perfusion failure. As a minimum in a mid window, it is unlikely to add strong predictive signal for creatinine ratio.

Minimum diastolic pulmonary artery pressure late (25–36h) can reflect RV failure/venous congestion and low forward flow, which can impair renal perfusion. It is a moderately proximal hemodynamic signal but indirect for AKI and can be confounded by ventilator/PEEP and volume status.

Count/frequency of PA diastolic pressure measurements in the immediate postop window mainly reflects monitoring intensity (e.g., presence of PA catheter, clinician concern). It is not a physiologic renal mechanism and is heavily proxy-biased.

Measurement frequency of PA diastolic pressure during 13–24h is primarily a care-process/acuity proxy rather than a direct renal perfusion or injury marker. Per rules, _measured variables have low renal specificity.

More frequent PA diastolic monitoring at 25–36h may indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability closer to the creatinine outcome. Still, it is largely clinician-behavior/monitoring intensity rather than a direct kidney-relevant physiologic value.

Mean heart rate in the first 12h reflects immediate postoperative stress, pain, pacing/arrhythmias, and resuscitation. It is an indirect and often transient correlate of perfusion/illness severity with limited renal specificity early after surgery.

Mean heart rate at 13–24h may reflect sustained sympathetic drive, arrhythmia burden, or evolving shock, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion. However, the mean can wash out brief but important episodes and remains nonspecific.

Mean heart rate at 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine and may capture sustained tachycardia from low cardiac output, sepsis, or ongoing vasopressor needs affecting renal perfusion. Still indirect and less informative than extremes or direct renal biomarkers.

Peak heart rate in the immediate postop period often reflects transient agitation, pain, pacing, or early arrhythmias. It is a weak-to-moderate indirect signal for renal risk because early peaks are common and not kidney-specific.

Peak heart rate during 13–24h can indicate clinically meaningful tachyarrhythmia or persistent stress/shock, which can reduce renal perfusion. Extremes are more informative than means, but the link to AKI remains indirect.

Peak heart rate at 25–36h is a nearer-term marker of ongoing hemodynamic stress (e.g., low output, sepsis, AF with RVR) that can precede worsening renal function. It is still nonspecific and may reflect treatment effects (inotropes) rather than renal injury itself.

Minimum heart rate early may reflect sedation, hypothermia, conduction disease, or pacing, and only indirectly relates to renal perfusion. Early postoperative bradycardia is common and not strongly predictive of later creatinine rise.
Minimum heart rate at 13–24h may capture bradyarrhythmias or excessive beta-blockade/pacing issues that could reduce cardiac output. Renal relevance is plausible but weak and nonspecific.

Minimum heart rate at 25–36h could reflect clinically significant bradycardia/low output states closer to the outcome window, potentially impairing renal perfusion. Still an indirect proxy and less kidney-specific than blood pressure, urine output, or creatinine trends.
Heart rate measurement frequency in 0–12h mainly reflects routine ICU monitoring and documentation patterns rather than physiology. It has high proxy penalty and minimal renal specificity.
Pulse measurement frequency at 13–24h is largely a monitoring/acuity proxy and typically near-universal in ICU patients. It provides little incremental information about renal injury pathways.
Pulse measurement frequency at 25–36h remains primarily a care-process variable with substantial proxy bias. It is not a direct marker of renal perfusion or evolving AKI and should be low importance.

Mean inspired O2 fraction/oxygen as an anesthetic carrier gas in the immediate postop window. It has no direct renal specificity and mainly reflects ventilator/anesthesia practice rather than AKI physiology.
Peak inspired O2 use early after surgery. High FiO2 is a nonspecific marker of respiratory support and is not a strong mechanistic predictor of creatinine rise at 60h.
Lowest inspired O2 requirement in the first 12h. This is largely unrelated to renal perfusion/injury pathways and is dominated by routine weaning/ventilation decisions.
Count/frequency of recorded O2/anesthesia gas values, which primarily reflects monitoring/documentation intensity. Per rules, _measured is a proxy feature with minimal renal relevance.
Measurement frequency of inspired O2/anesthesia gas in the 13–24h window. Still mainly a care-process/monitoring proxy and not a kidney-specific signal.
Measurement frequency of inspired O2/anesthesia gas closest to the outcome window. Despite temporal proximity, it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy without direct linkage to evolving AKI.
Mean nitrous oxide exposure early after surgery. N2O use is largely anesthetic practice-related and has no established direct prognostic relationship to postoperative creatinine ratio.
Peak nitrous oxide exposure in the first 12h. This is not a recognized AKI pathway marker and is unlikely to add predictive value beyond general anesthesia context.
Lowest nitrous oxide exposure early postop. It is clinically remote from renal injury mechanisms and mostly reflects whether/when N2O was used.
Frequency of N2O documentation/measurement in 0–12h. This is a monitoring/documentation proxy and should score very low for renal outcome prediction.
Frequency of N2O documentation/measurement in 13–24h. It is unlikely to reflect renal perfusion or injury and mainly captures care-process variation.
Frequency of N2O documentation/measurement in 25–36h. Even near the outcome window, it remains a weak proxy feature with no kidney specificity.
Mean medical air as a carrier gas/ventilator gas mix in the immediate postop period. This is not kidney-specific and is driven by ventilator/anesthesia settings rather than AKI risk.
Maximum air fraction/use early after surgery. Extremes here do not map to a renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic pathway and are largely redundant with FiO2 management.
Minimum air fraction/use in 0–12h. This is a nonspecific ventilation/anesthesia parameter with minimal relevance to predicting creatinine ratio at 60h.
Count/frequency of recorded air/anesthesia gas values in 0–12h. As a _measured variable it mainly reflects monitoring intensity and offers little renal prognostic information.

Measurement frequency of delivered air as an anesthetic/ventilator gas variable is largely a documentation/monitoring artifact. It has no direct renal specificity and mainly reflects care process rather than AKI physiology.

Counts/flags of air agent measurement are a monitoring-intensity proxy without mechanistic linkage to renal perfusion or injury. Even in the closer 25–36h window, it is unlikely to add meaningful signal for creatinine rise.

Average isoflurane exposure in the immediate postoperative period mostly reflects anesthesia/sedation practice and is temporally early when transient hemodynamic effects are common. It is not a direct renal biomarker and is an indirect proxy at best.

Peak isoflurane dose could correlate with episodes of vasodilation/hypotension that may reduce renal perfusion, but this is indirect and confounded by clinical management. Early (0–12h) peaks are often transient and less predictive of Hour-60 creatinine than later hemodynamics.

Minimum isoflurane dose is not a meaningful marker of renal risk and is heavily driven by sedation targets and weaning rather than kidney physiology. The aggregation (min) does not capture renal insult pathways well.
Isoflurane 'measured' is primarily a documentation/monitoring frequency variable. It has high proxy penalty and minimal renal specificity for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Measurement frequency of isoflurane in 13–24h reflects ongoing sedation/ventilation management and clinician behavior more than renal pathophysiology. Not a direct or strong precursor of AKI.
Even close to the outcome window, isoflurane measurement frequency is a care-process proxy and not a physiologic renal signal. Per rules, _measured variables should score very low.

Mean mixed/central venous O2 saturation reflects global oxygen delivery/consumption balance and can indirectly relate to renal hypoperfusion. In 0–12h it is often influenced by bypass/anesthesia and resuscitation, so predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine is limited.

Mean venous O2 saturation in 13–24h captures the recovery vs persistent low-output trajectory, which is more prognostically relevant to renal perfusion than the immediate postop period. Mean may still dilute brief shock episodes, keeping it moderate rather than high.

High venous O2 saturation is often nonspecific (e.g., high FiO2, reduced extraction, sedation) and does not directly indicate renal injury. Early postoperative maxima are particularly confounded and weak for AKI prediction.

A maximum venous O2 saturation is generally not a renal-risk signal and may reflect measurement conditions rather than improved perfusion. It has low renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity to creatinine rise.

Minimum venous O2 saturation can indicate episodes of low cardiac output/shock with reduced oxygen delivery, a plausible AKI pathway via renal hypoperfusion. However, in 0–12h it is often transient and influenced by immediate postop factors, so not top-tier.

Low nadir venous O2 saturation in 13–24h suggests persistent or recurrent inadequate oxygen delivery/low-output state, closely linked to renal hypoperfusion and evolving AKI risk. The 'min' aggregation appropriately captures clinically important extremes.

Frequency of SvO2 measurement is mainly a proxy for hemodynamic concern and monitoring intensity rather than a renal mechanism. It may weakly signal instability in the early postop period but remains indirect.

SvO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h can reflect ongoing shock/low-output evaluation, which is indirectly related to AKI risk. Still, it is a clinician-behavior proxy and should remain low importance.

Indicates whether mixed/central venous O2 saturation was checked, usually in patients with shock/low cardiac output monitoring. It is a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than a renal mechanism; timing is close to outcome but still indirect.

Average maintenance IV fluid volume in the immediate postop window reflects resuscitation/volume strategy more than kidney injury itself. Early fluids are common and confounded by clinician practice; mean may dilute brief hypoperfusion/overload signals.

Mean maintenance IV volume during the trajectory phase may relate to ongoing hemodynamics and fluid balance, which can influence renal perfusion/congestion. Still largely treatment-driven and indirect for AKI prediction.

Mean maintenance IV volume closer to Hour 60 can reflect persistent need for fluids or inability to transition to enteral intake, sometimes correlating with ongoing instability affecting kidneys. However it remains a clinician-behavior proxy and mean aggregation can miss extremes.

Peak maintenance IV volume early after surgery may capture aggressive fluid administration, but this period is heavily influenced by routine postop protocols and transient physiology. It is an indirect signal with substantial confounding by practice patterns.

A high peak maintenance IV volume in 13–24h may indicate ongoing instability or attempts to support perfusion, which can precede AKI. Max better captures clinically meaningful exposure than mean, but remains treatment/proxy-heavy.

A high peak maintenance IV volume in the 25–36h window may signal persistent hemodynamic issues or fluid management challenges that can affect renal perfusion/congestion near the outcome. Still indirect and confounded by clinician choice, so not top-tier.

Minimum maintenance IV volume in the immediate postop period is often near zero due to intermittent charting or protocolized holds and is not a clear renal-pathway marker. Poor aggregation relevance and high noise make it weak for predicting creatinine rise.

Minimum maintenance IV volume is typically driven by documentation gaps or brief pauses rather than meaningful renal physiology. It provides little specific information about renal perfusion or evolving AKI.
Minimum maintenance IV volume late in the first 36h is still hard to interpret physiologically and is often a charting artifact. It has low renal specificity and weak prognostic proximity.

Represents whether maintenance IV volume was recorded, which mainly reflects documentation/monitoring rather than physiology. As a _measured feature in the immediate postop window, it is a weak proxy for renal risk.
Measurement indicator for maintenance IV volume is primarily a care-process variable with minimal renal specificity. It adds little beyond general ICU documentation intensity.
Whether maintenance IV volume was measured/recorded is a monitoring proxy and not a mechanistic driver of AKI. Even close to outcome, _measured variables should remain low importance.

Indicates whether systolic pulmonary artery pressure was measured via PA catheter, usually in higher-acuity or RV/low-output concern patients. This is an indirect severity/monitoring proxy with limited renal specificity, especially early postop.

PA systolic pressure measurement suggests ongoing invasive hemodynamic monitoring for instability that can affect renal perfusion. Still a _measured proxy rather than a physiologic value, so only weak-to-moderate usefulness.

Late PA pressure measurement implies persistent need for invasive monitoring and possible ongoing cardiogenic shock/congestion risk relevant to AKI. However it remains a monitoring-intensity indicator, so importance is capped low.

Indicates whether pulmonary artery diastolic pressure was recorded (monitoring intensity). This is a care-process proxy with weak, indirect linkage to AKI risk and should not strongly predict Hour-60 creatinine.
Binary flag for PAP diastolic being measured, reflecting catheter presence/acuity rather than renal physiology. At best an indirect severity marker, so low value for predicting creatinine_ratio.

Measurement flag for PAP diastolic in the closest window may correlate with ongoing hemodynamic instability requiring PA catheterization. Still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy, so only weak predictive utility.
Binary indicator that CVP was measured, mainly reflecting line placement and monitoring intensity. It does not directly encode renal perfusion or injury and is heavily proxy-prone.
CVP measurement presence is a nonspecific marker of ICU management rather than kidney function. Any association with AKI is indirect and confounded by illness severity.

CVP measurement in the 25–36h window may signal persistent shock/volume management concerns closer to the outcome. However it remains a monitoring proxy (not the CVP value), so predictive strength is limited.

Mean end-tidal CO2 reflects ventilation/perfusion and cardiac output indirectly, which can relate to renal perfusion. In the immediate post-op period it is heavily influenced by ventilator settings/anesthesia, reducing specificity for AKI prediction.
Average EtCO2 over 13–24h may reflect evolving perfusion/ventilation status and thus indirect renal hypoperfusion risk. Mean aggregation can wash out short low-flow episodes and remains nonspecific.

Sustained lower EtCO2 in the 25–36h window can indicate ongoing low cardiac output or poor pulmonary perfusion, which can precede AKI. Still indirect and influenced by ventilation, but timing improves prognostic value.

Peak EtCO2 early post-op is usually driven by ventilator adjustments, CO2 production, or transient hypoventilation rather than renal mechanisms. The extreme high value is not a strong AKI pathway signal.
Maximum EtCO2 is a nonspecific respiratory/ventilation marker and not closely tied to renal injury pathways. High extremes are less informative for renal hypoperfusion than low extremes.
Late-window peak EtCO2 still mainly reflects ventilation/hypoventilation episodes and is not a direct precursor of AKI. Limited renal specificity and high proxy/setting dependence.
Minimum EtCO2 can capture brief low perfusion/low cardiac output states that may threaten renal perfusion. In 0–12h, transient post-bypass/anesthesia effects are common, so signal-to-noise is modest.

Low EtCO2 episodes in 13–24h can indicate low cardiac output or severe V/Q mismatch, both associated with renal hypoperfusion risk. Using the minimum better captures clinically meaningful insults than the mean.

Minimum EtCO2 in the closest window may reflect ongoing low-flow physiology preceding creatinine rise, making it a moderate near-term predictor. It remains indirect and confounded by ventilator management, limiting it below top-tier.

Frequency/presence of EtCO2 measurement is primarily a monitoring/ventilation context indicator rather than physiology. As a _measured feature it is proxy-prone and should contribute little to predicting creatinine_ratio.

Count/frequency of end-tidal CO2 documentation in 13–24h, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and ventilation status. It has no direct renal specificity and is largely a care-process proxy, so it adds little to predicting creatinine rise.

Frequency of ETCO2 measurement in 25–36h may weakly proxy ongoing ventilatory support/instability closer to the outcome window. Still primarily monitoring behavior with low renal mechanistic linkage, so only slight predictive value.

Average FiO2 in the immediate postoperative period reflects oxygenation/respiratory support needs and overall acuity. Renal relevance is indirect (hypoxemia/shock correlation) and early means can wash out transient insults.

Peak FiO2 early after surgery captures episodes of severe oxygenation impairment or high support. This is still an indirect proxy for systemic illness rather than a kidney-specific pathway, and early postoperative derangements are common/transient.
Minimum FiO2 in 0–12h mostly reflects periods of stability or weaning rather than renal risk. It has low renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity to Hour-60 creatinine.
FiO2 measurement frequency in 0–12h is largely driven by routine ventilator charting and clinician workflow. It is a monitoring-intensity proxy with minimal direct renal relevance.
FiO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h primarily reflects documentation/monitoring patterns rather than physiology. It has low renal specificity and is not a strong near-term AKI signal.

FiO2 measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing ventilatory dependence/instability closer to the creatinine outcome. However it remains a care-process proxy, so importance is limited.

Mean tidal volume early post-op reflects ventilator strategy and body size more than renal physiology. Any association with AKI is indirect (severity/ARDS correlation) and early averages dilute brief insults.
Mean tidal volume in 13–24h is mainly a ventilation management parameter with weak, nonspecific links to renal outcomes. It is not a direct marker of renal perfusion or injury.

Mean tidal volume in 25–36h can proxy ongoing mechanical ventilation and potential ventilator-induced hemodynamic effects closer to Hour 60. Still indirect and heavily confounded by clinician settings, so modest importance.

Maximum tidal volume in 0–12h reflects ventilator settings/episodes of higher volumes, often protocol- or size-driven. Renal linkage is indirect and early postoperative variability limits prognostic value.
Peak tidal volume in 13–24h is primarily a management/strategy signal rather than kidney-specific physiology. It offers little direct information about evolving AKI risk.

Peak tidal volume in 25–36h may capture periods of higher intrathoracic pressure/ventilatory demand that could correlate with hemodynamic compromise near the outcome window. Nonetheless it remains an indirect, clinician-driven proxy.
Minimum tidal volume in 0–12h generally reflects low-support periods or ventilator adjustments and is not kidney-specific. It has minimal mechanistic connection to creatinine rise.
Minimum tidal volume in 13–24h is largely a ventilator-setting artifact and weakly related to renal perfusion or injury. Predictive utility for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is minimal.

Minimum delivered tidal volume in the 25–36h window reflects ventilator strategy/respiratory mechanics rather than kidney physiology. It may weakly proxy severity (e.g., ARDS, sedation) that can correlate with AKI, but renal specificity is low.

Indicator of whether tidal volume was recorded in the immediate postop period, mainly reflecting monitoring/documentation intensity. It has minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion or evolving AKI and is heavily proxy-prone.

Measurement/recording frequency for tidal volume in 13–24h is a care-process signal rather than a renal mechanism. At best it weakly tracks illness severity, so predictive value for creatinine ratio is low.

Whether tidal volume is being measured/recorded at 25–36h may indicate ongoing mechanical ventilation and higher acuity closer to the outcome window. Still primarily a monitoring/proxy variable with low renal specificity.

Whether arterial pulse pressure is measured/recorded early postop mainly reflects presence of an arterial line and monitoring intensity. It is not a physiologic value and is therefore a weak, proxy-heavy predictor of AKI.

ABP pulse measurement at 13–24h suggests continued invasive monitoring and potentially ongoing hemodynamic concern. This is still a proxy (not the pulse pressure itself), so renal relevance remains indirect.

Continued ABP pulse measurement at 25–36h implies persistent invasive monitoring near the outcome window, which can correlate with shock/vasopressor needs that affect kidneys. However it remains a clinician-behavior proxy rather than a direct renal/perfusion metric.
Heart rate being measured/recorded in 0–12h is routine in ICU care and mostly reflects documentation rather than physiology. It provides little specific information about renal injury risk.
Heart rate measurement/recording in 13–24h is ubiquitous and primarily a monitoring-intensity feature. It has minimal renal specificity and high proxy penalty.

Heart rate measurement/recording at 25–36h remains a near-universal monitoring artifact rather than a physiologic extreme or sustained exposure. As a proxy variable it is unlikely to add meaningful signal for creatinine ratio.

Whether mean arterial pressure is measured/recorded early postop suggests an arterial line and hemodynamic monitoring, which can correlate with shock risk. Still, it is not the MAP value and is therefore an indirect proxy with limited renal specificity.

MAP measurement/recording at 13–24h may indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability monitoring, modestly associated with renal hypoperfusion risk. Proxy penalty remains substantial because it captures monitoring rather than hypotension itself.

Continued MAP measurement/recording at 25–36h (closest window) can be a stronger signal of persistent hemodynamic concern, which is mechanistically linked to AKI after cardiac surgery. Despite being a proxy variable, timing makes it a moderate predictor.

Respirations measured/recorded in the immediate postop period largely reflects routine monitoring and documentation. It has low renal specificity and does not capture a direct perfusion or kidney-injury pathway.

Respiratory rate measurement/recording in 13–24h is mainly a care-process variable and weakly related to renal outcomes. Any association would be via nonspecific illness severity rather than kidney physiology.

Respirations measured/recorded at 25–36h may indicate ongoing ventilatory support/acuity closer to the creatinine outcome. However it remains a monitoring/documentation proxy with indirect renal relevance.

Indicates whether pulse oximetry was recorded in the immediate postop period, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity. It has no direct renal specificity and is a weak proxy for acuity, so it is unlikely to add signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Measurement flag for pulse oximetry in the 13–24h window, largely a care-process/monitoring variable. Minimal mechanistic linkage to AKI beyond nonspecific severity/attention.

Measurement flag for pulse oximetry in the closest window to outcome, but still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Even with temporal proximity, it lacks renal specificity and should not meaningfully predict creatinine rise.

Likely indicates whether tube/enteral intake was documented early after surgery, reflecting feeding status and documentation rather than renal physiology. Any association with AKI would be indirect and confounded by general illness severity.
Documentation/measurement of tube intake in the trajectory phase, mostly a proxy for care progression and stability. Weak and nonspecific relationship to renal perfusion or kidney injury.
Measurement of tube intake in the 25–36h window is still primarily a process-of-care marker. Despite being temporally closer, it does not directly encode renal injury or perfusion failure.

Average maintenance/bolus IV fluid volume early postop may reflect resuscitation needs and fluid loading that can influence renal perfusion and congestion. However, the mean can wash out short hypotensive/resuscitation episodes and is confounded by clinician behavior.

Peak IV bolus/maintenance volume in the first 12h can capture aggressive resuscitation for shock/low output states, which are linked to AKI risk. Still early and partly treatment-driven, but the extreme is more informative than the mean/min.

Minimum IV bolus/maintenance volume in the first 12h is hard to interpret clinically and does not capture renal insults well. It is largely redundant/noisy and heavily influenced by ordering/documentation patterns.
A measurement/recording indicator for IV bolus volume, primarily reflecting documentation intensity rather than physiology. Per rules, _measured variables are weak proxies and not renal-specific.
Measurement flag for IV bolus volume in 13–24h, again mostly a care-process variable. It provides little direct information about renal perfusion or evolving AKI.
Measurement flag for IV bolus volume in the leading-indicator window, but still a documentation proxy. Temporal proximity does not overcome the lack of renal specificity for a _measured feature.

Average pacemaker use/setting early postop suggests conduction issues or low cardiac output support, which can relate to renal hypoperfusion risk. However, immediate postop pacing is common/transient and is an indirect proxy for hemodynamics.

Persistent pacing requirement in 13–24h may better indicate ongoing cardiac dysfunction and reduced renal perfusion reserve. It remains indirect but has more prognostic value than the immediate 0–12h window.

Ongoing pacing in 25–36h is temporally close and may reflect sustained low output/bradyarrhythmia management affecting renal perfusion. Still not kidney-specific and can be confounded by clinician thresholds, so not top-tier.

Any pacemaker use in the first 12h (binary max) captures whether pacing was needed at all, a coarse marker of perioperative cardiac instability. It is early and nonspecific for renal injury, so only modest predictive value.

Represents presence/use of temporary pacing in the 13–24h window, a marker of conduction issues and postoperative cardiac dysfunction. Only indirectly relates to AKI via low cardiac output/hemodynamic instability and is a care-context proxy rather than a renal-specific signal.

Temporary pacing in the 25–36h window may indicate ongoing cardiac dysfunction that could impair renal perfusion. Still nonspecific and largely a proxy for illness/cardiac status rather than a direct renal injury marker.
Temporary pacing in the immediate 0–12h postoperative period is common and often transient after cardiac surgery. Weak renal specificity and high confounding by routine postoperative management.

Ongoing need for pacing in 13–24h suggests persistent hemodynamic/cardiac issues that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. However it remains indirect and not a strong standalone predictor of creatinine rise.

Persistent pacing requirement closer to the outcome window can reflect continued low-output physiology affecting kidney perfusion. Predictive value is limited by low renal specificity and being a treatment/monitoring proxy.

Measurement frequency/documentation of pacemaker status in 0–12h mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine postoperative charting. Minimal direct linkage to renal function and strong proxy penalty.
How often pacemaker status is recorded in 13–24h is primarily a clinician-behavior/monitoring signal. It does not capture renal physiology and is unlikely to add beyond the binary pacing indicators.

More frequent pacemaker documentation in 25–36h may weakly signal ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy and not a renal mechanism, so capped at low importance.

Recording SLED/CRRT blood pump rate implies renal replacement therapy context, which is highly related to kidney dysfunction, but this feature is only a 'measured' frequency/availability indicator. Early window and proxy nature limit usefulness for predicting the hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Measurement of CRRT/SLED pump rate suggests ongoing RRT and severe AKI risk, but the variable reflects documentation/monitoring rather than delivered dose or renal function. Moderate proxy penalty keeps importance low.

In the 25–36h leading window, evidence of active RRT monitoring strongly implies evolving/severe kidney injury that will affect the hour-60 creatinine ratio. Still a 'measured' proxy (not actual clearance/dose), so not scored higher.

Sevoflurane exposure/recording is primarily an anesthesia management variable and is not a strong AKI pathway driver in this context. As a 'measured' feature in the immediate postoperative period, it is mostly process-related noise.

Documentation of sevoflurane use in 13–24h is uncommon and likely reflects charting artifacts or prolonged sedation context rather than renal physiology. Minimal renal specificity and high proxy penalty.

Sevoflurane measurement in 25–36h is unlikely to be causally tied to creatinine rise and mainly reflects care-process/documentation. Poor renal specificity and 'measured' aggregation support a minimal score.

Frequency of IV intake measurement reflects fluid management attention and early resuscitation, which can relate indirectly to renal perfusion and congestion. However it is a monitoring-intensity proxy (not actual volume balance), and early postoperative variability reduces signal.

Measurement frequency of IV intake in 13–24h may weakly indicate ongoing fluid titration in response to instability that can affect kidneys. Still largely a documentation proxy rather than a direct exposure (volume/FB), so limited importance.

Counts/flags whether IV intake was recorded in the 25–36h window, reflecting monitoring/documentation intensity more than physiology. Only weakly related to renal outcomes via fluid management context, so proxy penalty keeps it low.

Indicates whether right-sided drain output was measured early post-op, largely a care-process/monitoring variable. It has minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion or AKI pathways and is far from the Hour 60 creatinine outcome.

Measurement indicator for drain output in the 13–24h window, mainly reflecting clinician attention rather than a renal mechanism. Drain monitoring frequency is not a specific predictor of creatinine rise.

Measurement indicator for drain output in the closest (25–36h) window; may weakly proxy ongoing bleeding/instability prompting closer monitoring. Still primarily a process variable with low renal specificity, so it remains low.

A binary indicator that an arrival-time field was recorded, which is administrative/operational rather than physiologic. It provides essentially no renal-specific information for predicting Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Administrative measurement flag unrelated to kidney function or renal perfusion. Any association would be incidental and dominated by proxy/recording effects.
Administrative/measurement indicator with no plausible mechanistic connection to AKI or creatinine trajectory. Temporal proximity does not help because the content is not physiologic.

Proportion of time in normal sinus rhythm early post-op; arrhythmias can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion but this is indirect. Mean in 0–12h may wash out brief hemodynamic insults and early transient rhythm issues are common.

Average sinus rhythm burden in 13–24h may reflect recovery vs persistent arrhythmia affecting perfusion, but remains an indirect proxy for renal hypoperfusion. Mean aggregation reduces sensitivity to short impactful episodes.

Sustained sinus rhythm in the 25–36h window is a closer indicator of stable cardiac output/perfusion leading into the Hour 60 creatinine measurement. Still indirect (not a renal biomarker), but timing improves prognostic value.

Binary max suggests sinus rhythm occurred at least once in 0–12h, which is not clinically discriminative and poorly captures arrhythmia burden. Minimal renal specificity and high redundancy with other rhythm summaries.
Presence of any sinus rhythm during 13–24h is a weak, non-specific indicator and does not quantify hemodynamic impact. Limited renal relevance and redundant with mean/min rhythm features.

Having any sinus rhythm in 25–36h is still too coarse to reflect sustained perfusion stability or arrhythmia-related shock. Despite temporal proximity, the max/binary aggregation is low quality for renal prediction.

Binary min implies whether the patient was continuously in sinus rhythm throughout 0–12h; loss of continuous sinus rhythm can proxy early instability. However early post-op rhythm disturbances are common and indirect for AKI risk.

Continuous sinus rhythm across 13–24h may indicate more stable hemodynamics than intermittent arrhythmia, but remains an indirect predictor of renal outcomes. Useful but modest given lack of direct renal specificity.

Continuous sinus rhythm in 25–36h is a stronger near-term marker of hemodynamic stability and renal perfusion leading up to Hour 60. Still not kidney-specific, so it does not reach top-tier importance.

Count/frequency of documenting normal sinus rhythm in the immediate postop window; largely reflects monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. Very indirect link to AKI risk and early epoch is noisy.
Measurement frequency of normal sinus rhythm during 13–24h is primarily a care-process/monitoring proxy. Only weak, nonspecific association with renal outcomes.

Later measurement frequency of normal sinus rhythm may weakly proxy ongoing instability/arrhythmia surveillance closer to the outcome. Still predominantly a monitoring-intensity signal, not a renal mechanism.
Average burden of sinus rhythm variants early after surgery is common and often transient. It is not kidney-specific and mean aggregation further dilutes brief hemodynamic effects relevant to AKI.

Persistent sinus rhythm variants in the trajectory phase could weakly reflect autonomic stress or evolving instability that may affect renal perfusion. Still indirect and mean aggregation is not ideal for capturing impactful episodes.

Sinus rhythm variant burden closer to Hour 60 may modestly correlate with ongoing physiologic stress, but remains a nonspecific proxy for renal hypoperfusion. Mean aggregation likely underweights short clinically important events.

Any occurrence of sinus rhythm variant in the first 12h may indicate transient conduction/physiologic stress but is common post-bypass. Indirect renal relevance and early timing limit predictive value.

Presence of sinus rhythm variants during 13–24h may signal incomplete recovery or instability that could contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still a weak, nonspecific predictor without direct renal linkage.

Any sinus rhythm variant in the 25–36h window is closer to the creatinine outcome and may better reflect ongoing hemodynamic vulnerability. Still indirect (not kidney-specific), but max/any-occurrence captures clinically meaningful episodes better than mean.

Minimum of a binary/indicator for sinus rhythm variant is typically uninformative (often just reflects at least one timepoint without the variant). Provides little renal-specific or prognostic signal, especially early.
Minimum of the sinus-variant indicator is largely redundant and poorly aligned with capturing harmful extremes. Weak and indirect relationship to renal outcomes.
Minimum of a binary rhythm-variant indicator remains low-information and redundant with max/mean. Not kidney-specific despite being closer in time.
Frequency of documenting sinus rhythm variants is mainly a monitoring/clinician-attention proxy. Minimal direct renal relevance and early postop period is confounded by routine surveillance.
Measurement count for sinus rhythm variants reflects monitoring intensity more than physiology. Only weakly related to AKI risk.
Later increased rhythm-variant measurement may proxy concern for instability closer to the outcome. Still primarily a care-process variable, so capped at low importance.

Mean burden of atrial arrhythmia (e.g., AF/flutter) early postop can reduce cardiac output and renal perfusion and is associated with complications. However, early timing and mean aggregation (vs capturing sustained/high-rate episodes) temper its predictive strength.

Represents burden of atrial arrhythmia during 13–24h, which can correlate with hemodynamic instability and congestion. Renal link is indirect and mean burden is a nonspecific severity proxy rather than a direct AKI pathway signal.

Atrial arrhythmia burden in the 25–36h leading window may reflect ongoing low output or need for rate/rhythm control. Still an indirect predictor of renal dysfunction and largely a general instability marker rather than kidney-specific.

Any atrial arrhythmia in the immediate 0–12h period is common post-cardiac surgery and often transient. It may signal early instability but has weak renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Presence of atrial arrhythmia at any point in 13–24h suggests persistent postoperative dysrhythmia and possible hemodynamic compromise. Renal relevance remains indirect and not a strong standalone AKI precursor.

Atrial arrhythmia occurring in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect ongoing low cardiac output, venous congestion, or vasopressor exposure that can worsen renal perfusion. Still not kidney-specific, but timing makes it a more useful predictor than earlier windows.

Minimum of a binary arrhythmia indicator is typically uninformative (often just indicates whether arrhythmia was absent at least once). It adds little renal-specific or prognostic information and is redundant with max/mean.
Binary minimum mainly reflects that arrhythmia was not continuously present, which is not a clear renal mechanism. Redundant with max/mean and weakly related to AKI risk.

Binary minimum does not capture clinically meaningful extremes or sustained exposure relevant to renal perfusion. It is largely redundant and provides minimal incremental value for predicting creatinine ratio.
Measurement count/frequency is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. It has no direct renal specificity and early postoperative monitoring is routine.
Represents how often atrial arrhythmia status was recorded, reflecting clinician attention/telemetry documentation patterns. This is a weak, nonspecific proxy with little direct linkage to AKI.
In the 25–36h window, increased measurement frequency could reflect concern for instability, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still primarily a care-process proxy, so capped at low importance.

Average paced rhythm burden early after surgery often reflects conduction issues or temporary pacing use, which may correlate with low output states. Renal association is indirect and early postoperative pacing is common, limiting specificity.

Paced rhythm burden in 13–24h may indicate persistent conduction dysfunction and potential hemodynamic compromise. However it remains a nonspecific proxy for illness severity rather than a direct renal injury marker.

Persistent pacing requirement in 25–36h can signal ongoing low cardiac output or conduction failure closer to the creatinine outcome, which can impair renal perfusion. Still indirect, but timing improves prognostic proximity.
Any paced rhythm in 0–12h is common postoperatively and may not indicate sustained hemodynamic compromise. Renal relevance is indirect and early timing is less predictive for Hour-60 creatinine.

Presence of pacing at any point in 13–24h suggests ongoing conduction support and possible instability. It is still a proxy variable with limited renal specificity and unclear causal proximity to AKI.

Represents presence of a paced rhythm during the 25–36h window, suggesting conduction disease or postoperative pacing dependence. Renal relevance is indirect (hemodynamic/acuity proxy) and not a specific AKI pathway, though later timing modestly increases prognostic value.

Indicates whether paced rhythm occurred in the immediate 0–12h postoperative period. Early pacing is common and often transient after cardiac surgery, making it a weak and nonspecific proxy for later creatinine rise.

Captures paced rhythm presence in the 13–24h trajectory phase, which may reflect persistent conduction issues and ongoing hemodynamic vulnerability. Still indirect for kidney injury and largely a severity/procedure proxy rather than renal-specific signal.

Captures paced rhythm presence closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome, potentially reflecting ongoing low-output risk or conduction instability. However it remains an indirect marker with substantial proxy penalty and limited renal specificity.

Measurement count/frequency for paced rhythm documentation in 0–12h primarily reflects monitoring intensity and charting practices. It has high proxy penalty and minimal mechanistic linkage to AKI, so predictive value should be low.

Measurement frequency in 13–24h is mainly a care-process/monitoring proxy rather than physiology. Even if increased monitoring correlates with instability, it is too indirect to be a strong predictor of creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency in 25–36h may weakly signal clinician concern or ongoing instability closer to the outcome. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score above low importance.

Mean burden of heart block in 0–12h reflects early postoperative conduction disturbance that can affect cardiac output. Renal impact is indirect and early abnormalities are often transient, limiting usefulness for Hour-60 creatinine.

Mean heart block burden in 13–24h may indicate persistent conduction disease with potential low-output physiology. Association with AKI is plausible but nonspecific, and mean aggregation can dilute brief but important episodes.

Mean heart block burden in 25–36h is closer to the outcome and may reflect sustained conduction-related low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion risk. Still indirect (not a renal biomarker), but sustained late instability can be a moderate predictor.

Any occurrence of heart block in 0–12h (max) captures an extreme event but in the immediate postoperative period it is common and may resolve quickly. Renal linkage is indirect via hemodynamics, so predictive value is limited.

Any heart block episode in 13–24h suggests ongoing conduction instability beyond the immediate postoperative phase. This can be a moderate predictor through association with low-output states and vasopressor need, though still not kidney-specific.

Any heart block episode in 25–36h is a late, clinically meaningful extreme that may signal persistent low-output physiology and renal hypoperfusion risk. It remains an indirect predictor with some proxy penalty but has better temporal proximity.

Minimum heart block indicator in 0–12h is typically redundant (often just reflects absence at some point) and poorly captures clinically relevant extremes. It has weak renal specificity and low aggregation quality for AKI prediction.

Minimum heart block indicator in 13–24h is largely uninformative and redundant compared with max/mean, since it can be zero even with important episodes. Limited renal relevance and poor aggregation for capturing insult.

Minimum heart block indicator in 25–36h does not capture the presence or severity of conduction events and is dominated by max/mean features. Despite closer timing, it has low aggregation quality and minimal renal-specific information.

Count/frequency of documenting heart block in the immediate postop window; this is primarily monitoring intensity/annotation rather than physiology. Weak and indirect renal linkage, and early epoch is noisy.
Measurement frequency of heart block during 13–24h mainly reflects surveillance/clinical attention. Only an indirect severity proxy with little renal specificity.

Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy ongoing instability prompting closer monitoring, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a clinician-behavior signal (_measured) with low renal specificity.

Average presence of ischemic ST changes early after surgery suggests myocardial ischemia but is often transient and treatment-related. Renal impact is indirect via potential low cardiac output/hypoperfusion, and mean can wash out brief insults.

Persistent/average ischemic ST changes in the trajectory phase can indicate ongoing myocardial ischemia and risk of low output, which is a recognized AKI pathway. Mean still dilutes extremes but sustained ischemia is clinically meaningful.

Sustained ischemic ST changes closer to the outcome window can signal ongoing cardiac dysfunction and renal hypoperfusion risk. Still indirect (not a renal biomarker) and mean aggregation may miss short severe episodes.

Any occurrence (max) of ischemic ST change in the immediate postop period may reflect transient ischemia during emergence/resuscitation. Indirect renal relevance and early timing limit predictive value.

Any ischemic ST change during 13–24h suggests clinically significant ischemia beyond the immediate postop noise, raising risk for low cardiac output and AKI. Max is the better aggregation for capturing an ischemic insult.

Any ischemic ST change in 25–36h is a strong near-term signal of ongoing myocardial ischemia/instability that can drive renal hypoperfusion and worsening creatinine by 60h. Max captures the presence of an acute insult better than mean.

Minimum of a binary ischemic ST-change indicator is typically uninformative (often just reflects at least one timepoint without ischemia). Poor aggregation for capturing renal-relevant ischemic burden, especially early.
Binary min mainly indicates whether ischemia was absent at any point, which does not track ischemic burden or hypoperfusion risk well. Low renal specificity and redundant with max/mean.
Binary min is a weak summary that fails to capture episodes of ischemia that matter for renal perfusion. Even in the closest window it is dominated by the more informative max/mean features.

Frequency of ST-change measurement/documentation is largely a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than a physiologic renal mechanism. Early postop measurement patterns are especially driven by routine protocols.
More frequent ST-change documentation may reflect clinician concern for ischemia/instability, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still a proxy variable (_measured) with limited renal specificity.

Increased measurement frequency late (25–36h) may indicate ongoing instability prompting closer surveillance, modestly associated with AKI risk. However it remains clinician-behavior driven and should not score high.

Average burden of non-specified rhythm abnormalities early postop is a nonspecific marker of cardiac instability. Renal relevance is indirect via hemodynamics, and early mean aggregation is noisy and diluted.

Represents proportion of time in a non-standard rhythm (e.g., paced, junctional, ectopy) during 13–24h, which may correlate with hemodynamic instability. Renal relevance is indirect and the mean can dilute brief low-output episodes, so predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is limited.

Non-standard rhythm burden in the 25–36h window may reflect ongoing cardiac dysfunction affecting renal perfusion. Still an indirect proxy and mean aggregation may wash out short but important low-output periods, so only weak-to-moderate usefulness.

Indicates whether any 'other' rhythm occurred in the immediate postoperative period, often transient after bypass/anesthesia. Early timing is noisy and rhythm category is not kidney-specific, so it is only a weak indirect predictor.

Any occurrence of an 'other' rhythm in 13–24h may signal evolving conduction/low-output issues that can reduce renal perfusion. Still nonspecific and largely a proxy for overall instability rather than a direct AKI pathway.

Any 'other' rhythm in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may capture clinically meaningful ongoing hemodynamic compromise. While still indirect, the near-term timing and extreme/any-occurrence aggregation make it a moderate predictor.

A minimum/absence indicator for 'other' rhythm in 0–12h is not clinically informative for renal risk and is largely redundant with max/mean rhythm features. It has minimal mechanistic linkage to AKI and early postoperative rhythms are often transient.
Minimum/absence of 'other' rhythm in 13–24h provides little actionable signal and is redundant with max/mean rhythm burden. Renal relevance is indirect and weak.

Minimum/absence of 'other' rhythm in 25–36h is not a meaningful marker of renal perfusion or injury and is redundant with the corresponding max feature. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is minimal.

Counts/reflects how often the 'other rhythm' variable was recorded, which mainly captures monitoring/documentation intensity. This is a care-process proxy with little direct renal specificity, especially in the immediate postoperative window.

Measurement frequency of rhythm categorization is primarily a proxy for clinician attention/monitoring rather than kidney physiology. It has low renal specificity and limited incremental value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
More frequent rhythm documentation in 25–36h may reflect concern for instability that could affect renal perfusion. Still largely a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should remain low importance.

Represents use/burden of ASV ventilator mode early post-op, which is mainly a respiratory management choice. It is not kidney-specific and is heavily influenced by clinician practice and immediate postoperative routine.
ASV mode burden in 13–24h reflects ventilator strategy and weaning trajectory more than renal physiology. Any renal association is indirect (severity/prolonged ventilation), so importance is minimal.
Any ASV use in 0–12h is largely a routine ventilator setting choice after cardiac surgery. It is a weak proxy for illness severity and has little direct linkage to AKI mechanisms.
Any ASV use in 13–24h mainly reflects respiratory management and local practice patterns. Renal relevance is indirect and nonspecific, so it is low importance.
Minimum/absence of ASV use in 0–12h is not a meaningful physiologic marker and is dominated by clinician choice. It provides negligible information about renal perfusion or evolving kidney injury.

ASV ventilator mode presence is mainly a respiratory management choice and only indirectly relates to renal outcomes via overall illness severity. The 13–24h timing is moderately informative, but this is a high proxy/low renal-specificity feature.
Measurement frequency of ASV mode is primarily monitoring/documentation intensity rather than physiology. Early (0–12h) postoperative ventilation patterns are common and nonspecific for AKI risk.
How often ASV mode is recorded reflects care processes and attention rather than a kidney injury pathway. Even in 13–24h, this remains a weak, indirect proxy for severity.

Recording frequency of ASV mode is still a proxy, but at 25–36h it may weakly signal ongoing respiratory failure/ICU complexity closer to the creatinine outcome. Per rules, _measured features should remain low importance.

Mean time in controlled ventilation in the immediate postoperative window mainly reflects routine post-cardiac surgery management and sedation needs. It is only indirectly linked to AKI through severity/hemodynamics and is prone to proxy penalty.

Sustained controlled ventilation at 13–24h can indicate slower recovery and higher acuity, which correlates indirectly with AKI risk. Still not renal-specific and the mean aggregation is a coarse proxy for severity.

Any use of controlled ventilation early after surgery is common and not specific for renal hypoperfusion or injury. As a binary extreme it captures presence, but remains largely a nonspecific severity marker.

Controlled ventilation persisting into 13–24h suggests ongoing critical illness and may correlate with AKI risk via shock/ventilator dependence. However it is still an indirect proxy without direct renal mechanism.

Minimum (i.e., whether controlled mode was ever absent/present depending on encoding) in 0–12h is hard to interpret clinically and likely redundant with other controlled-mode indicators. It has low renal specificity and high proxy penalty.
This minimum aggregation is less clinically meaningful than a clear presence/absence indicator and is redundant with other controlled-mode summaries. Renal linkage is indirect and weak.
Frequency of documenting controlled ventilation is a monitoring/care-process signal rather than a physiologic driver of AKI. In the immediate postoperative period it is especially nonspecific.
Documentation frequency of controlled mode reflects clinician behavior and charting intensity more than renal risk. Despite better timing than 0–12h, it remains a weak proxy feature.

At 25–36h, frequent documentation of controlled ventilation may weakly indicate ongoing instability closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Still, as a _measured variable it is primarily proxy information and should stay low.
SIMV mode use in 0–12h largely reflects routine ventilator weaning strategy and sedation practices. It has minimal direct connection to renal perfusion or kidney injury pathways.

SIMV use at 13–24h may indicate partial weaning vs ongoing respiratory failure, indirectly tracking overall recovery trajectory. Renal relevance remains indirect and the mean aggregation is not kidney-specific.

Any SIMV use in the immediate postoperative period is common and primarily reflects ventilator management rather than renal pathophysiology. As a binary presence indicator it adds little specific signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

SIMV use in the 13–24h window is mainly a marker of ongoing ventilatory support/illness severity rather than a renal-specific pathway. It may correlate indirectly with hemodynamic instability and AKI risk but is a weak proxy.
Early (0–12h) SIMV exposure is common post-op and heavily influenced by routine anesthesia/ICU practice. It is nonspecific and temporally distant from Hour 60 creatinine changes.
Persistent need for SIMV in 13–24h can reflect slower recovery and higher acuity, which can associate with AKI risk. Still, it is a care-process proxy with limited renal specificity.
Count/frequency of SIMV documentation in 0–12h primarily reflects monitoring/charting patterns rather than physiology. _measured variables are weak proxies and not renal-specific.
Measurement frequency of SIMV in 13–24h is mostly clinician/monitoring intensity and EMR workflow. It provides little direct information about renal perfusion or evolving AKI.
In 25–36h, frequent SIMV documentation may indicate ongoing ventilator dependence closer to the outcome window. Still, it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.
Spontaneous mode early after surgery largely reflects extubation readiness and routine weaning practices. It has minimal direct linkage to kidney injury mechanisms and is temporally early.
More spontaneous ventilation in 13–24h can indicate clinical recovery and less shock, indirectly lowering AKI risk. However, it is still nonspecific and influenced by sedation/weaning protocols.
Any spontaneous mode in the immediate 0–12h period is often protocol-driven and variable across clinicians. It is a weak, indirect proxy for renal outcomes.

Achieving spontaneous mode at any point in 13–24h suggests improving cardiopulmonary status, which can correlate with better renal perfusion. The association is indirect and confounded by care decisions.
Minimum spontaneous mode in 0–12h mostly captures that many patients are fully supported early post-op. It is nonspecific and not a strong predictor of later creatinine rise.

Lack of spontaneous breathing throughout 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness and potential hemodynamic compromise, indirectly increasing AKI risk. Still, it is not renal-specific and is care-process influenced.
Frequency of documenting spontaneous mode in 0–12h is primarily a charting/monitoring artifact. It does not directly encode renal perfusion or kidney injury.
This _measured feature is mainly a proxy for documentation intensity and ventilator management attention. It has low renal specificity and limited prognostic proximity.

Closer to Hour 60, documentation frequency of spontaneous mode may weakly reflect ongoing respiratory recovery vs dependence, which correlates with overall stability. Nonetheless it remains a monitoring-intensity proxy, limiting importance.

Mean arterial pressure in the immediate post-op period reflects systemic perfusion pressure, a key determinant of renal blood flow and AKI risk. Using the mean may dilute brief hypotensive insults and early post-bypass variability, so it is a moderate (not top-tier) predictor.

Mean MAP in the 13–24h window reflects overall perfusion pressure during the early recovery/trajectory phase. Renal perfusion is relevant to AKI risk, but the mean can wash out hypotensive episodes and this epoch is not closest to Hour 60.

Mean MAP in the 25–36h window captures sustained perfusion status closer to the creatinine outcome. Persistent lower MAP near this time is a strong precursor of renal hypoperfusion, though mean is still less sensitive than minima.

Peak MAP in the immediate 0–12h postoperative period often reflects transient hypertension from pain, agitation, or vasoactive titration. It has weak mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine rise compared with hypotension-related metrics.

Maximum MAP in 13–24h is generally a nonspecific signal (e.g., episodic hypertension) and is not a well-established driver of AKI after cardiac surgery. Limited renal specificity and high redundancy with other MAP summaries.

Maximum MAP in 25–36h is usually not the clinically relevant extreme for renal injury risk (hypotension is). Despite temporal proximity, it remains a weak and nonspecific predictor of creatinine ratio.

Minimum MAP in 0–12h captures hypotensive episodes immediately after surgery/CPB that can contribute to renal ischemia. However, early transient hypotension is common and may be less prognostic than later sustained instability.

Minimum MAP in 13–24h reflects clinically meaningful hypotension during the trajectory phase when recovery vs deterioration becomes apparent. Hypotensive nadirs are a strong precursor to AKI via renal hypoperfusion.

Minimum MAP in 25–36h is a near-term indicator of renal hypoperfusion closest to the Hour 60 creatinine outcome. Hypotensive extremes are highly mechanistically linked to evolving AKI and are well-captured by the minimum aggregation.

Count/frequency of MAP measurements is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. In 0–12h, arterial monitoring is routine post-cardiac surgery, so it adds little renal-specific signal.
MAP measurement frequency mainly reflects nursing/clinician behavior and device presence rather than perfusion. By 13–24h it may weakly track instability, but remains a low-specificity proxy.

In 25–36h, continued frequent MAP documentation may indicate ongoing hemodynamic instability or continued arterial line use, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still largely a care-process/monitoring proxy, so capped low.
Proportion of time NPO in 0–12h is routine immediately after cardiac surgery and mostly reflects standard postoperative care. It has minimal direct renal specificity for predicting creatinine ratio.

Remaining NPO in 13–24h may reflect delayed extubation or higher acuity, but it is an indirect proxy with weak mechanistic linkage to kidney injury. Limited added value for Hour 60 creatinine beyond more direct physiologic variables.

Persistently NPO in 25–36h can signal ongoing critical illness (e.g., prolonged ventilation, ileus) that correlates with AKI risk. However it is nonspecific and largely a proxy for severity/care trajectory rather than renal mechanism.
Any NPO status in 0–12h is expected postoperatively and provides little discrimination. It is a care-process variable with minimal renal specificity.
Any NPO status in 13–24h is common and mainly reflects feeding/airway management decisions rather than kidney physiology. Weak, indirect association with creatinine change and high proxy penalty.

NPO status in the 25–36h window mainly reflects feeding readiness/clinical stability and care plan rather than a renal mechanism. It may weakly proxy severity or ongoing instability that can correlate with AKI risk, but is indirect.

Early immediate post-op NPO is routine after cardiac surgery and has little specificity for kidney injury. The minimum over 0–12h is unlikely to capture meaningful renal risk beyond standard practice.
Persisting NPO in 13–24h can reflect slower recovery, but remains a nonspecific care-process marker with weak renal linkage. The 'min' aggregation adds little clinically relevant signal.
Being NPO as late as 25–36h may indicate ongoing critical illness, ventilation, or hemodynamic concerns that can associate with AKI. Still, it is an indirect proxy and not kidney-specific.
Measurement frequency of NPO status is primarily documentation/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. In 0–12h it is especially routine and unlikely to add predictive value for creatinine rise.
This is a monitoring/documentation proxy with minimal renal specificity. Any association with AKI would be indirect via overall ICU complexity rather than a causal pathway.

More frequent documentation of NPO status in 25–36h could weakly reflect ongoing instability or clinician concern, which may correlate with AKI risk. However it remains a process/monitoring proxy, so it should stay low.

Average diet status in the immediate post-op period largely reflects routine NPO/slow feeding advancement and is not kidney-specific. Mean aggregation further dilutes any brief clinically meaningful changes.
Diet advancement over 13–24h is a general recovery marker with weak and indirect linkage to renal outcomes. The mean is a coarse proxy and not a strong near-term AKI indicator.

Diet status averaged over 25–36h may reflect overall recovery vs persistent critical illness, which can correlate with subsequent creatinine rise. Still, it is nonspecific and heavily influenced by clinician practice.
Any diet given in 0–12h is uncommon and mostly reflects local practice rather than renal physiology. It has minimal mechanistic connection to AKI at 60h.
Reaching any diet by 13–24h can indicate faster recovery and less severe illness, indirectly associated with lower AKI risk. It remains a nonspecific proxy and not a direct renal predictor.
Having diet by 25–36h suggests clinical stability and may inversely correlate with AKI risk, but the relationship is indirect and practice-dependent. Not kidney-specific enough for a higher score.
Minimum diet status in 0–12h will usually be NPO and reflects routine post-op management. It provides little discriminatory information for predicting creatinine ratio.
Minimum diet status in 13–24h is still largely driven by standard feeding protocols and non-renal factors (airway, nausea, procedures). It is a weak proxy for renal outcomes.

Remaining at the lowest diet level (often NPO) in 25–36h can reflect ongoing critical illness that increases AKI risk. However it is indirect and confounded by care decisions, so importance stays low.

Frequency/indicator that a diet order/type was documented in the first 12h; largely a care-process/monitoring artifact. Minimal direct renal specificity and high proxy penalty for workflow rather than kidney physiology.
Diet-type documentation frequency in 13–24h reflects feeding/order activity more than renal function. Weak, indirect association with AKI risk and mostly clinician-behavior signal.

Diet-type documentation frequency in 25–36h may weakly proxy clinical stability/readiness to feed closer to the outcome window. Still nonspecific with substantial proxy penalty and no direct kidney mechanism.
Proportion/average time classified as an 'other' diet type early post-op is not kidney-specific. In 0–12h it mainly reflects perioperative NPO/ordering patterns rather than renal perfusion or injury.
Mean 'other' diet status in 13–24h is a nonspecific nutrition/order category. Any link to creatinine trajectory is indirect and likely confounded by overall recovery and care practices.
Mean 'other' diet status in 25–36h could weakly reflect ongoing critical illness or inability to advance diet nearer to Hour 60. Still a low-specificity proxy with limited mechanistic linkage to AKI.
Binary indicator that 'other' diet occurred at any point in 0–12h; extremes here are not clinically meaningful for renal injury. Mostly reflects ordering/NPO status immediately after surgery.
Any occurrence of 'other' diet in 13–24h is a weak, nonspecific marker of feeding/order status. High proxy penalty and little renal specificity.
Any occurrence of 'other' diet in 25–36h may weakly signal persistent instability or delayed recovery closer to the creatinine outcome. Still indirect and not a renal biomarker.
Binary indicator that 'other' diet was present for the entire 0–12h window (or never absent) is not kidney-specific. Early post-op diet patterns are dominated by routine NPO/care pathways.
Sustained 'other' diet status in 13–24h remains a nonspecific care-process variable. Limited prognostic proximity to AKI compared with hemodynamics, urine output, or renal labs.
Sustained 'other' diet status in 25–36h could modestly proxy ongoing critical illness near the outcome window. However it remains indirect with substantial confounding and low renal specificity.
Measurement/documentation frequency of the 'other' diet category in 0–12h is primarily monitoring intensity/workflow. No direct renal pathway and strong proxy penalty.
Documentation frequency of 'other' diet in 13–24h reflects charting/ordering rather than kidney function. Weak and indirect association with creatinine change.
In 25–36h, more frequent diet documentation might weakly correlate with instability or active management closer to Hour 60. Still a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Average time in regular cardiac rhythm early post-op may indirectly relate to renal perfusion via arrhythmias/hemodynamic instability. In 0–12h it is often transient and nonspecific, so predictive value for Hour 60 creatinine is limited.

Represents proportion of time in a regular rhythm during 13–24h (recovery/trajectory phase). Rhythm regularity is only an indirect proxy for hemodynamic stability and perfusion, with limited renal specificity and substantial redundancy with irregularity indicators.

Proportion of time in regular rhythm in the 25–36h leading-indicator window. Closer timing helps, but regular rhythm itself is not kidney-specific and mainly reflects general stability rather than a direct AKI pathway.

Binary indicator that the patient ever had regular rhythm in the immediate 0–12h postoperative period. This is common and nonspecific after surgery/anesthesia and provides little direct information about renal perfusion or evolving AKI.

Whether regular rhythm occurred at any point in 13–24h. The 'max/ever' aggregation is not very informative (most patients will have some regular rhythm) and is an indirect proxy with weak renal linkage.

Whether regular rhythm occurred at any point in 25–36h. Even near the outcome window, an 'ever regular' flag is low-signal and not renal-specific; more informative would be sustained irregularity or hemodynamic variables.

Binary indicator that rhythm was regular for the entire 0–12h window (i.e., no irregularity). Continuous regularity may weakly suggest hemodynamic stability, but early postoperative rhythm disturbances are common and renal specificity remains low.
Indicates sustained regular rhythm throughout 13–24h. Persistent regularity can reflect recovery and fewer low-output episodes, but it remains an indirect predictor with modest renal relevance.

Indicates sustained regular rhythm throughout 25–36h, closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Persistent regularity may better capture absence of AF/arrhythmia-related low cardiac output and renal hypoperfusion, though still indirect.
Count/frequency of documentation/measurement of regular rhythm in 0–12h. This is primarily monitoring intensity/EMR process signal rather than physiology, with minimal renal specificity.
Measurement frequency of regular rhythm in 13–24h. Mostly reflects documentation/monitoring patterns and clinician workflow, not a mechanistic renal risk factor.
